# Bronchoalveolar lavage in interstitial lung disease

# Ulrich Costabel, MD, FCCP,* and Josune Guzman, MD†

Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) has become a standard diagnostic procedure for the majority of patients with interstitial disease. The technique is safe, minimally invasive, and reveals specific information in some disorders such as pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis, alveolar hemorrhage, malignant infiltrates, or dust exposure. Here BAL can often replace lung biopsy. The results of BAL cell differentials with a lymphocytic, a neutrophilic, an eosinophilic, or a mixed cellular pattern can be used as an adjunct to diagnosis. If a BAL finding is compatible with the suspected diagnosis in the context of an appropriate disease history and clinical and radiologic findings, this can then be sufficient for disease confirmation. Recent research focused on the pathogenesis of various types of interstitial lung disorders. In this regard, BAL findings contributed to the characterization of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) as a condition with a predominant T-helper-2 (Th2) cytokine profile, whereas BAL findings in sarcoidosis and hypersensitivity pneumonitis are characterized by a Th1-dominant profile. The clinical value of BAL to assess the activity of disease processes and to provide prognostic information is still under debate. The routine performance of serial BAL is not recommended at present. Curr Opin Pulm Med 2001, 7:255–261 © 2001 Lippincott Williams & Wilkins, Inc.

Even less invasive than BAL is the technique of induced sputum. Its diagnostic role in interstitial lung disease, particularly in comparison with BAL, has not yet been well defined, however. Further studies are needed in this area [2•,3].

# Recent guidelines and recommendations

Detailed guidelines and recommendations regarding the technical aspects of BAL were published by scientific societies some 10 years ago [4–6]. Recently, a comprehensive European Respiratory Society (ERS) Task Force Report [7,8] updated the available recommendations for the standardization and measurement of acellular components of BAL including different categories of specific components such as pulmonary surfactant, immunoglobulines, proteases and antiproteases, angiotensin-converting enzyme, antioxidants, oxidants and oxidation products, lipid mediators, cytokines, soluble adhesion molecules, markers of fibrosis, granulocyte derived markers, tumor markers, and markers of cell death. In addition, this field has been extended by the recent publication of the ERS Task Force Report on BAL in children [9]. Technical aspects are slightly different in children (eg, adapting the instillation volume to different ages and sizes of children), the use of different sizes of bronchoscopes, and the different normal values for cells.

*Department of Rheumatology and Allergy, Ruhrlandklinik, Essen; and †General and Experimental Pathology, University of Bochum, Germany.

Bronchoalveolar lavage studies should not be limited to counting the cell differentials. At least as important as examining cell differentials is observing the morphologic appearances of cells and particles. Examples are the different morphology in extrinsic allergic alveolitis (foamy macrophages, heterogenous macrophage size, presence of plasma cells) versus that of sarcoidosis (more monomorphous appearance of macrophages, less activated lymphocytes), the presence of malignant cells, the characteristic features of alveolar proteinosis, or the detection of dust particles such as asbestos bodies in occupational exposure conditions. In some of the rarer lung diseases, BAL has a high diagnostic value and can replace lung biopsy.

These guidelines set up a framework that defines the performance of BAL and the laboratory processing and analysis of the recovered constituents. There is a general agreement that the instillation volume should approximate 100 to 300 mL sterile saline in 20- to 50-mL aliquots, and that, in diffuse lung disease, the middle or lingular lobe is used as a standard site of BAL. It is beyond the scope of this review to describe in detail the bronchoscopic procedure and laboratory processing of the recovered fluid.

In other settings, BAL is not specific but can be used as an adjunct to diagnosis. There are many diseases with a lymphocytic, a neutrophilic, an eosinophilic, or a mixed cellular pattern. In patients with these diseases, it is important to keep in mind that BAL cell differentials should not be used as an isolated finding for making a diagnosis but should always be interpreted in the context of disease history and clinical, laboratory, and radiologic findings. Combining BAL with high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) can enhance diagnostic accuracy.

In general, the diagnostic value of acellular components of BAL still remains to be proven. A recent study found clinical significance for monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1) levels in BAL, with significantly and markedly higher levels in IPF patients than in other types of ILD, with almost no overlap [19]. Confirmation by others is needed.

It is still not established whether BAL is clinically useful for assessing the activity of disease processes and for providing prognostic information. It is also not proven that BAL is better than other parameters to guide therapy. In this regard, serial BAL to monitor the course of a disease cannot be routinely recommended at present.

# Recent research findings

# Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis

Several papers have recently highlighted the prognostic importance of the new histologic classification of the idiopathic interstitial pneumonia, which limits the definition of IPF to the histopathologic pattern of usual interstitial pneumonitis (UIP) and discriminates several other subgroups with a better prognosis, including nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) [20–23]. The limited data available on BAL cell differentials in NSIP consistently show that this entity is characterized by a predominant lymphocytosis in BAL in addition to a mild increase in neutrophils and eosinophils [21,22,24•,25] (Table 4).

The BAL pattern seems similar to bronchitis obliterans with organizing pneumonia (BOOP) and contrasts with the predominant neutrophil and eosinophil increase in UIP or excessive increase of neutrophils in acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP), thus allowing a certain discrimination between favorable prognostic entities (such as BOOP and NSIP) and the more unfavorable diagnoses (such as UIP or AIP). This is in line with the older observations that a lymphocytic alveolitis in IPF may be.

Even in patients where lavage is not diagnostic but normal, it can be used to exclude certain diagnoses (eg, extrinsic allergic alveolitis, eosinophilic pneumonia, alveolar hemorrhage) and focus attention on other directions. The value of additional determination of the CD4/CD8 ratio in lavage samples has been disputed recently, because of the observed high variability of this ratio in sarcoidosis [14]. Nevertheless, three independent research groups have found almost identical values for sensitivity and specificity of elevated CD4/CD8 ratios for diagnosing sarcoidosis [15–17]. Whereas the sensitivity of an elevated ratio higher than 3.5 or 4.0 is only 55% because of the high variability, the specificity is high, at 95%. It is even higher than for transbronchial biopsy in one of these studies [16]. Whereas an elevated CD4/CD8 ratio would obviate biopsy in the appropriate clinical setting, this occurs in only approximately 50% of the patients with sarcoidosis because of the low sensitivity. Thus sarcoidosis cannot be excluded if the CD4/CD8 ratio is normal or even decreased below 1.0 [18].

pages.md:
# 258 Interstitial lung disease

associated with a more favorable response to treatment type II pneumocytes in the lungs. Both TF and TFPI [5]. Today, we can assume that these patients in former studies did not have the true IPF/UIP but rather had NSIP. IPF is a condition that seems to be characterized by a predominant expression of T-helper-2 (Th2) cytokines as shown by immunostaining and in situ hybridization of open lung biopsy specimen with positivity for IL-4 and IL-5 [26,27]. A recent study looked at BAL levels of IL-5 and granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor (GM-CSF), important growth factors for eosinophils, and found that IL-5 was high only in patients with eosinophilic pneumonia but not in patients with BOOP and IPF [28]. Granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor was detectable in a certain proportion of patients in each of the investigated disorders. The authors suggest that IL-5 is important in eosinophilic pneumonia, and GM-CSF in BOOP and IPF, for the recruitment of eosinophils to the lungs. However, their findings are inconsistent with those reported earlier by Walker et al. [29], who demonstrated elevated IL-5 levels in both eosinophilic pneumonia and IPF. Another recent paper evaluated the cell surface antigen CD30, a member of the TNF-receptor superfamily, reported to be expressed by blood CD4+ and CD8+ lymphocytes secreting Th2-type cytokines in vitro, in various interstitial lung diseases. This study found no correlation between this Th2 cell marker and the IL-5 levels and concluded that CD30 does not appear to be a useful marker for Th2 lymphocytes in BAL from patients with interstitial lung disease [30].

Hyperplastic type II pneumocytes are a characteristic feature of IPF and other interstitial disorders. In patients with diffuse alveolar damage, including those with acute interstitial pneumonia, these cells may be seen on BAL cell differentials. In IPF, they usually adhere to the basement membrane and are not found in the BAL cytology specimen. In this regard, a marker of epithelial hyperplasia or injury in the BAL fluid would be of interest. Recent papers described soluble, epithelial cell–derived markers such as cytokeratine-19 (CK19) and the carbohydrate antigen CA19-9 as elevated in BAL fluid in patients with IPF, fibrosing alveolitis associated with collagen vascular disease, and most markedly in acute interstitial pneumonia, but not in sarcoidosis [31–33]. IL-18 is produced by epithelial cells, activated macrophages, and lymphocytes and acts in synergy with IL-12, augmenting the interferon-g production [46] and thus further promoting the Th1 response. Serum IL-18 levels significantly correlated with serum interferon-g levels and were higher in those patients with a positive Ga-67 lung scan, thus indicating the potential clinical usefulness of IL-18 as a marker of disease activity [45].

# Sarcoidosis

Sarcoidosis has been recognized as a granulomatous disease characterized by dominant expression of Th1 cytokines such as IL-2 and interferon-g, with low expression of Th2 cytokines such as IL-4 and IL-5 [29,36,37], as shown by studies of BAL cells and fluid. Recently, two independent research groups studied the cytokine expression of blood and BAL lymphocytes at the single-cell level. They consistently demonstrated that a clear Th1 cytokine profile was observed not only in CD4+ but also in CD8+ BAL T-lymphocytes in patients with pulmonary sarcoidosis [38•,39]. Interleukin-12, produced by activated alveolar macrophages, is a key cytokine in the shift of the immune response toward the Th1 profile and has been found to be upregulated in the lungs and lymph nodes of patients with sarcoidosis [36,40–43]. More recently, IL-18 has been found to be increased in the airway epithelial cells of patients with sarcoidosis [44] and to be elevated in the BAL fluid of patients with sarcoidosis as well [45•,46].

Importantly, the levels of CK19 and CA19-9 correlated with the number of neutrophils, and CK19 also correlated with eosinophils, indicating that these markers may be an index of the severity of the inflammation and the ongoing epithelial injury. Purified CA19-9 had a chemotactic activity for neutrophils in vitro [33]. Thus, CA19-9 released from regenerating alveolar epithelial cells may be at least in part responsible for the neutrophil recruitment to the alveolar space in IPF. Tissue factor (TF) and tissue factor pathway inhibitor (TFPI) antigens are also mainly localized in hyperplastic immunoreactive thioredoxin and IL-2 receptors in.

Serum IL-18 levels significantly correlated with serum interferon-g levels and were higher in those patients with a positive Ga-67 lung scan, thus indicating the potential clinical usefulness of IL-18 as a marker of disease activity [45].
items.value: Hyperplastic type II pneumocytes are a characteristic feature of IPF and other interstitial disorders. In patients with diffuse alveolar damage, including those with acute interstitial pneumonia, these cells may be seen on BAL cell differentials. In IPF, they usually adhere to the basement membrane and are not found in the BAL cytology specimen. In this regard, a marker of epithelial hyperplasia or injury in the BAL fluid would be of interest. items.md: # Sarcoidosis
items.value: Sarcoidosis

items.md: Sarcoidosis has been recognized as a granulomatous disease characterized by dominant expression of Th1 cytokines such as IL-2 and interferon-g, with low expression of Th2 cytokines such as IL-4 and IL-5 [29,36,37], as shown by studies of BAL cells and fluid. Recently, two independent research groups studied the cytokine expression of blood and BAL lymphocytes at the single-cell level. They consistently demonstrated that a clear Th1 cytokine profile was observed not only in CD4+ but also in CD8+ BAL T-lymphocytes in patients with pulmonary sarcoidosis [38•,39]. Interleukin-12, produced by activated alveolar macrophages, is a key cytokine in the shift of the immune response toward the Th1 profile and has been found to be upregulated in the lungs and lymph nodes of patients with sarcoidosis [36,40–43]. More recently, IL-18 has been found to be increased in the airway epithelial cells of patients with sarcoidosis [44] and to be elevated in the BAL fluid of patients with sarcoidosis as well [45•,46].

The problem with reliable clinical markers for disease activity in sarcoidosis is still under debate [48]. Several recent BAL studies described new potential markers that were significantly different in patients with active and inactive sarcoidosis or that were thought to be useful prognostic factors. These markers include the expression of natural killer cell inhibitory receptors on T cells [49], the expression of angiotensin II receptors on BAL macrophages [50], the interleukin-1 receptor antagonist/interleukin-1 ratio [51], and the levels of clara cell 10-kda (CC10) protein in serum and BAL fluid [52]. In contrast to early reports, a recent study confirmed that an increased BAL CD4/CD8 ratio is seen in a subgroup of patients with sarcoidosis associated with a favorable prognosis. This subgroup consists of patients with the HLA-DR 17+ genotype [53]. This subgroup also had lower serum angiotensin-converting enzyme levels, and the majority presented with Lofgren syndrome. This confirms previous findings that the CD4/CD8 ratio is particularly high in Lofgren syndrome. Whether increased numbers of neutrophils in BAL fluid can reliably distinguish patients with sarcoidosis who experience remission from those who deteriorate, as indicated by Drent et al. [54], has still to be confirmed by other groups. The future will show whether these newly tested disease markers prove useful for testing activity and prognosis in clinical practice.

# Hypersensitivity pneumonitis

Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) is characterized by the most striking lymphocytosis in the BAL fluid of all interstitial lung disease, usually with a relative predominance of CD8+ T cells [55]. The CD4+ T cells are also significantly elevated in absolute number. Animal models recently suggested that the disease is characterized by a Th1-type response. Schuyler et al. [56] reported that a Th1CD4+ cell line could adoptively transfer experimental HP in mice, and Gudmundsson et al. [57] reported that interferon-g is necessary for the development of HP, as IFN-g knockout mice are resistant to development of the disease. In addition, the exposure to IL-12 enhanced interferon-g production, whereas IL-10 ameliorated the severity of experimental HP in mice [58,59]. The first clinical study on the cytokine profile in HP patients confirmed those animal data and showed that clinical HP is also characterized by a Th1-dominant profile of BAL CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, as analyzed by intracellular cytokine detection at the single-cell level. There was a predominance of interferon-g–producing T cells, which could be further stimulated by the addition of recombinant IL-12 to the culture medium, whereas the addition of IL-10 reduced the production of interferon-g [60]. Tumor necrosis factor (TNF) is also an important cytokine in hypersensitivity pneumonitis. A recent paper showed that the BAL levels of the soluble TNF receptors 1 and 2 were increased in HP patients and correlated significantly with the BAL lymphocyte percentage. This suggested that these receptors, as counterregulators of TNF, may be involved in the pathogenesis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis [61]. However, this finding is not specific for HP. In active pulmonary tuberculosis, for instance, soluble TNF receptor levels in BAL are also increased [62].

Acute episodes of HP are associated with an influx of neutrophils into the lungs, which may cause oxidative stress. Behr et al. [63•] exposed farmers to a standardized hay provocation test and found that those patients with acute farmer’s lung had significantly decreased concentrations of oxidized and reduced glutathione in the BAL fluid 6 hours after this exposure, whereas asymptomatic farmers had increased glutathione levels, similar to healthy cigarette smokers. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis is rare in smokers. In this regard, the authors speculate that farmers who are able to upregulate the glutathione concentrations in their epithelial lining fluid may be protected against the development of hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Further trials are needed to confirm this hypothesis, which might explain why some individuals are susceptible to develop the disease.

Neutrophils and oxidative stress also play an important role in lung transplantation. Transplant recipients with bronchiolitis obliterans syndrome, thought to represent chronic rejection, have significantly elevated BAL levels of neutrophils, myeloperoxidase, IL-8, oxidized glutathione, and oxidized methionine residues [64,65]. Elevated levels of IL-8 and neutrophils in the BAL fluid of donor lungs are strongly associated with the development of severe early graft dysfunction [66].

# Alveolar proteinosis

Alveolar proteinosis is a rare condition characterized by the abnormal accumulation of surfactant-like phospholipids within the alveoli and distal airways. Bronchoalveolar lavage is not only important for diagnosis but also for treating this disorder by large volume lavage, the so-called whole-lung lavage. Recently, the importance of diagnostic BAL was illustrated in two cases studied [67]. In both, HRCT was characteristic of alveolar proteinosis and surgical lung biopsy was negative. In both cases, BAL showed the characteristic and diagnostic features, indicating that open-lung biopsy is not the diagnostic gold standard in this condition.

# Interstitial lung disease

teinosis have been shown to carry a serum autoantibody that inhibits GM-CSF function [69]. Other studies showed normal GM-CSF production by circulating monocytes and alveolar macrophages, suggesting no intrinsic abnormalities in GM-CSF signaling [70•]. This is of interest because recently the treatment of alveolar proteinosis with recombinant GM-CSF has been promoted but was not effective in all patients. Today, whole-lung lavage is still the established, safe, and effective standard treatment for this disorder.

This is the first report on increased levels of interleukin-18, the most recent cytokine related to a T-helper-1 immune response, in the BAL fluid of patients with pulmonary sarcoidosis. The authors speculate about a potential clinical usefulness of IL-18 as a marker of disease activity.

46 Shigehara K, Shijubo N, Ohmichi M, et al.: IL-12 and IL-18 are increased and stimulate IFN-g production in sarcoid lungs. J Immunol 2001, 166:642–649.

47 Koura T, Gon Y, Hashimoto S, et al.: Expression of thioredoxin in granulomas of sarcoidosis: possible role in the development of T lymphocyte activation. Thorax 2000, 55:755–761.

This is an excellent article showing that asymptomatic farmers have increased glutathione levels in the BAL fluid. A possible conclusion is that farmers who are able to upregulate glutathione in the lungs, may be protected against development of disease. This is an important contribution to the potential recognition of susceptibility factors in hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

65 Behr J, Maier K, Braun B, et al.: Evidence for oxidative stress in bronchiolitis obliterans syndrome after lung and heart-lung transplantation. Transplantation 2000, 69:1856–1860.

66 Fisher AJ, Donnelly SC, Hirani N, et al.: Elevated levels of interleukin-8 in donor lungs is associated with early graft failure after lung transplantation. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2001, 163:259–265.

67 Crocker HL, Pfitzner J, Doyle IR, et al.: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis: two contrasting cases. Eur Respir J 2000, 15:426–429.

68 Costabel U, Guzman J: Alveolar proteinosis. Eur Respir Mon 2000, 14:194–205.

69 Kitamura T, Tanaka N, Watanabe J, et al.: Idiopathic pulmonary alveolar proteinosis as an autoimmune disease with neutralizing antibody against granulocyte/macrophage colony-stimulating factor. J Exp Med 1999, 190:875–880.

70 Thomassen MJ, Yi T, Raychaudhuri B, et al.: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis is a disease of decreased availability of GM-CSF rather than an intrinsic cellular defect. Clin Immunol 2000, 95:85–92.

GM-CSF deficiency has been implicated as an important factor in the development of pulmonary alveolar proteinosis. This is the first study to demonstrate that circulating monocytes and alveolar macrophages from patients with alveolar proteinosis are able to produce GM-CSF and respond to GM-CSF, suggesting no intrinsic abnormalities in GM-CSF signaling. The disease is likely the result of decreased availability of the GM-CSF, because of blocking anti-GM-CSF antibodies.

items.md: This is an excellent article showing that asymptomatic farmers have increased glutathione levels in the BAL fluid. A possible conclusion is that farmers who are able to upregulate glutathione in the lungs, may be protected against development of disease. This is an important contribution to the potential recognition of susceptibility factors in hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

items.md: 70 Thomassen MJ, Yi T, Raychaudhuri B, et al.: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis is a disease of decreased availability of GM-CSF rather than an intrinsic cellular defect. Clin Immunol 2000, 95:85–92.
items.value: 70 Thomassen MJ, Yi T, Raychaudhuri B, et al.: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis is a disease of decreased availability of GM-CSF rather than an intrinsic cellular defect. Clin Immunol 2000, 95:85–92.

items.md: GM-CSF deficiency has been implicated as an important factor in the development of pulmonary alveolar proteinosis. This is the first study to demonstrate that circulating monocytes and alveolar macrophages from patients with alveolar proteinosis are able to produce GM-CSF and respond to GM-CSF, suggesting no intrinsic abnormalities in GM-CSF signaling. The disease is likely the result of decreased availability of the GM-CSF, because of blocking anti-GM-CSF antibodies.
